content.decision_question: Will contracting the state-sector selective track actually reduce net segregation, or will it simply relocate selective demand into a sector the state cannot regulate?
content.evidence_boundary: Further evidence — such as tracking non-state sector enrollment and composition changes following any state-sector contraction — could establish whether displacement occurs and at what magnitude. It cannot resolve, prior to such evidence, whether policy should extend jurisdiction over non-state providers, which is a governance-design and political-value choice.
content.tension: Any active contraction of the selective state track (via caps, thresholds, or structural reform) is bounded by jurisdiction: church, foundation, and private schools sit outside Klebelsberg Centre authority and could absorb displaced demand, meaning state-sector equity gains could be offset by expansion of an unregulated, less transparent, possibly more segregated channel.
content.title: Contraction location: state-sector control versus displacement to ungoverned channels
